title: 15 Yuan
subtitle: The worst mindset you can have as a seller

blocks.content: *Context: Over a period of more than a month in China, I kept a journal each day detailing what made me happiest that day as research for the happiness video I made. This is an excerpt from one of the daily journal entries I wrote (so there might be grammatical mistakes).*
*I had just spent two pages writing about a dilemma I had over eating crackers.*
Okay, with that philosophical rant itch I had scratched, let’s talk about the happiness moment.

We had just finished lunch in the city, coming from a museum about Confucius, and was catching the bus home. As we were walking, a salesperson started his predictable monologue. It was then my brother showed his weakness: interest. The salesperson pounced, going in for the deal.

Doing a simple marginal cost vs. marginal benefit calculation in my head, my gut immediately told me it was overpriced. Judging from my brother’s face, I knew it wasn’t that important of a purchase, just a small joyous addition that if he got, would’ve been nice, but if he didn’t, wasn’t that big of a deal. So we started walking away.

To a seasoned Chinese person (and I would guess, almost anyone who has been in an Asian country), this was a very regular interaction. It was pretty much the equivalent of the obligatory “how was your weekend?” question in the Western world. But as we walked, the salesperson called again: “15 yuan”.

In an instant, I noticed my grandpa’s body language change. It was subtle, but firm. He properly looked at the salesperson. Unconsciously intrigued, I began observing the salesperson too. In a span of less than 5 seconds I noticed a few things. The burning red that tainted the fingertips of the man. A little girl, who I imagined was his daughter, no older than 5 or 6, sitting on a chair, her hands wrapped around a phone. And finally, a look of desperation that escaped the usual stoic expressions etched onto the faces of all salespeople.

From my grandpa’s change in behaviour, I knew he arrived at the same feeling I did, only a few crucial seconds earlier: empathy. To much of my grandma’s chagrin, my grandpa walked over to my brother, who was standing right in front of the little store. He asked my brother what animal he wanted. And once my brother got the candy, paid the 15 yuan, double checking the money was sent to the man, and only leaving when the man confirmed the money had been received.
As we walked away, I asked my grandpa:
“您为什么对那个人这么好啊?”
Why were you so nice to that person?
To which he replied, “I wasn’t being nice to that person. Your brother seemed like he really wanted the candy.”
My grandpa waited a beat, and then added,
“The man lowered his price by 20 yuan.”
“So?”
“It means he sets his prices purely on his perceived emotions of the buyer. He isn’t confident in his labor. He doesn’t see his own value.”
“And that’s bad, right?”
“That’s the worst mindset you can have as a seller.”
His words stuck with me the whole entire day. But it was what he didn’t say that I will likely remember forever. Because by pairing his actions with his words, it revealed a deeper message.

Even for desperate sellers, to go immediately from 35 yuan to only 15 yuan was not ordinary. So unordinary that by simply hearing those two numbers, my grandpa changed his entire behaviour. The man had no confidence in his labor at all, relying entirely on what he thought the buyer would value this product. He went beyond a threshold of the usual desperation, and my grandpa sensed it. So, he made sure the man got the money.

My grandpa knew all too well what it was like to pass that threshold. And while he knew he couldn’t help everyone, he knew he could help this man. Perhaps I am reading too much into this interaction. But the fact that his actions could make me feel this way fills me with so much gratitude and happiness.
